Course Title: AI in Industrial Control Systems (ICS): New Risks
Category: Agriculture & Resources: Agriculture, Forestry, Fishing, Mining, Energy
Description: How AI introduces new attack surfaces in operational technology.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with industrial or operational environments (such as manufacturing, energy, or agriculture) and a general awareness of cybersecurity concepts will help you get the most from this course.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key ways in which AI introduces new attack surfaces within Industrial Control Systems (ICS) across sectors such as agriculture, energy, and mining. You will also be able to explain the potential risks these vulnerabilities pose to operational technology and apply foundational strategies to recognise and respond to AI-related threats in your working environment.

lessons.lesson title: AI in Industrial Control Systems (ICS): New Risks
lessons.Description: This lesson explores how the growing use of artificial intelligence within industrial control systems is creating new and often unexpected security risks for operational technology environments. As AI is increasingly embedded into critical infrastructure such as power grids, water treatment facilities, and manufacturing plants, it introduces attack surfaces that traditional cybersecurity approaches were never designed to address. We will look at the practical ways in which AI components can be targeted, manipulated, or exploited by malicious actors, and why these threats are particularly serious in environments where safety and reliability are paramount. By the end of this lesson, you will have a clearer understanding of the key vulnerabilities that AI brings to ICS environments and the steps organisations can begin to take to manage these risks responsibly.
lessons.content: How AI introduces new attack surfaces in operational technology.

slides.subtitle: Where operational technology meets artificial intelligence
slides.slidecontent:
• Industrial control systems (ICS) manage physical processes like power grids, water treatment, and manufacturing lines
• AI is increasingly embedded in these environments to optimise performance and predict faults
• New capability, new risk
• Every AI component added to an ICS creates a potential entry point for attackers
• Understanding these risks is the first step toward protecting critical infrastructure

slides.subtitle: Why traditional defences fall short against AI-powered attacks
slides.slidecontent:
• ICS were designed decades before AI existed, leaving security gaps that modern threats exploit
• AI enables dynamic, adaptive attacks that evade signature-based detection
• Attackers use AI to learn normal system patterns and disguise malicious activity as routine operations
• Legacy and new systems running side by side create unpredictable vulnerabilities
• Traditional security tools struggle to identify AI-driven attack patterns

slides.slidetitle: New Attack Vectors in AI-Enhanced ICS
slides.subtitle: How AI components expand the attack surface
slides.slidecontent:
• Machine learning models and data pipelines create entirely new entry points
• Training data corruption allows attackers to influence decisions before operators notice
• Remote connectivity for AI updates significantly widens exposure
• AI-powered anomaly detection tools can be manipulated to ignore genuine threats
• Each connected AI component multiplies potential vulnerabilities

slides.slidetitle: Critical AI Vulnerabilities in Industrial Settings
slides.subtitle: Where AI creates exploitable weak points
slides.slidecontent:
• Biased or corrupted training data leads to dangerous operational decisions
• Opaque AI models hide failures until catastrophic outcomes occur
• Sensor spoofing tricks AI systems with false environmental data
• Automated responses can trigger unpredictable cascading effects
• Black box algorithms make troubleshooting and forensics nearly impossible

slides.subtitle: Hidden threats in third-party AI components
slides.slidecontent:
• Pre-trained models may contain hidden backdoors or data poisoning
• Third-party libraries introduce unverified code into critical systems
• Vendor updates can silently introduce new vulnerabilities
• Many AI suppliers lack rigorous security audit processes
• Compromised shared libraries can affect multiple industrial systems

slides.subtitle: Real-world consequences of AI security failures
slides.slidecontent:
• Manufacturing disruption from manipulated production algorithms
• Power grid instability through compromised load balancing systems
• Water treatment contamination via altered chemical dosing controls
• Equipment damage from AI-driven maintenance schedule corruption
• Safety system failures when AI bypasses critical protection mechanisms

slides.subtitle: Practical strategies to protect AI-enhanced systems
slides.slidecontent:
• Segment AI components from core ICS networks using strict access controls
• Monitor AI model inputs and outputs for unusual patterns signaling manipulation
• Validate training data sources to prevent poisoned datasets
• Limit AI system privileges to prevent direct critical command execution
• Maintain regular patching schedules for all AI components and dependencies

slides.subtitle: Preparing your team for AI-related threats
slides.slidecontent:
• Train operations staff to recognise abnormal AI behaviour patterns
• Establish incident response procedures specific to AI security events
• Create documentation standards for AI system configurations and changes
• Develop testing protocols for AI component security before deployment
• Foster a security-first culture when adopting new AI technologies

scripts.script: Welcome to this course on AI security in industrial control systems. Industrial control systems, or ICS, are the technology backbone of our critical infrastructure - they manage power grids, water treatment plants, manufacturing facilities, and other systems that control real, physical processes. Increasingly, AI is being integrated into these environments to optimise performance, predict equipment failures, and improve operational efficiency. While this brings significant benefits, it also introduces new security risks. Every AI component added to an ICS creates potential entry points that attackers could exploit. Throughout this course, we'll explore what those risks look like and how to address them effectively.

Here's the fundamental challenge we face: industrial control systems were designed and built decades before artificial intelligence existed as a practical technology. This means their security architectures were never intended to defend against AI-powered threats. Traditional signature-based detection systems struggle with AI-driven attacks because these attacks can adapt and learn, making them appear like normal system behaviour. Attackers can use machine learning to study how your systems typically operate, then blend their malicious activities into those normal patterns. When you have legacy equipment running alongside modern AI-enabled systems, you create a complex environment where vulnerabilities can emerge in unexpected ways.

When we integrate AI into industrial control systems, we're not just adding functionality - we're creating entirely new pathways for potential attacks. Machine learning models require data pipelines, processing systems, and communication channels that didn't exist in traditional ICS environments. One particularly concerning attack vector is training data manipulation, where attackers corrupt the data used to teach AI systems, causing them to make poor decisions long before operators realise anything is wrong. The remote connectivity required for AI system updates and maintenance further expands the attack surface. Even more troubling, the AI tools designed to protect us - like anomaly detection systems - can themselves be fooled by sophisticated attackers.

Let's examine the specific vulnerabilities that AI introduces to industrial environments. Poor quality or deliberately corrupted training data can cause AI systems to make dangerous operational decisions. Many AI models operate as 'black boxes,' meaning their decision-making processes are opaque even to the engineers who deployed them. This makes it extremely difficult to identify when something has gone wrong until it's too late. Sensors feeding data to AI systems can be spoofed with false information, and because AI can trigger automated responses faster than humans can intervene, a single compromise can lead to cascading failures across multiple systems.

The supply chain presents another significant risk vector that many organisations overlook. When implementing AI in industrial settings, companies rarely build everything from scratch. Instead, they rely on pre-trained models, third-party libraries, and external components. These external elements can harbour hidden vulnerabilities - models trained on poisoned data, libraries with embedded backdoors, or code that hasn't undergone proper security review. Vendor updates, while necessary for functionality, can introduce new security flaws. Many AI suppliers simply don't have the rigorous security processes that critical infrastructure demands, yet their components become integral parts of your operational systems.

To understand why AI security matters so much in industrial settings, let's consider the real-world impacts of compromised AI systems. In manufacturing, manipulated production algorithms could disrupt entire supply chains or create defective products. Compromised AI in power grid management could destabilise electrical distribution across regions. Water treatment facilities with corrupted AI could result in contaminated water supplies. Equipment maintenance schedules manipulated by attackers could lead to costly breakdowns or safety hazards. Most critically, when AI systems that manage safety protocols are compromised, the consequences can be life-threatening.

Now let's focus on practical defence strategies. Network segmentation is crucial - isolate your AI components from core ICS networks using strict firewall rules and access controls. Implement comprehensive monitoring of AI model inputs and outputs to detect unusual patterns that might signal manipulation. Establish rigorous validation processes for training data to ensure it hasn't been corrupted. Apply the principle of least privilege to AI systems, ensuring they can't directly execute critical commands without human oversight. And maintain disciplined patching schedules for all AI components and their dependencies, treating them as the critical infrastructure components they've become.

Technology alone isn't sufficient - your people need to be prepared for AI-related security challenges. Train your operations staff to recognise when AI behaviour deviates from expected patterns, as they're often the first to notice something unusual. Develop specific incident response procedures for AI security events, which may require different approaches than traditional cybersecurity incidents. Create clear documentation standards for AI system configurations and ensure all changes are properly tracked. Establish thorough testing protocols to validate the security of AI components before they're deployed in production environments. Most importantly, foster a security-conscious culture that treats AI adoption as both an opportunity and a responsibility.

Who is this for: Plant managers, operations supervisors, facility engineers, OT technicians, industrial automation specialists, process control engineers, safety managers, maintenance supervisors
Team or Dept this is for: Operations, Engineering, Facilities Management, Safety, IT/OT Security, Industrial Automation
